title: Service Mesh
summary: Istio architecture, sidecar proxy pattern, mTLS, traffic management, observability, and when to use a service mesh.

sections.content:
## What is a Service Mesh?

A service mesh is an infrastructure layer that handles service-to-service communication. Instead of implementing cross-cutting concerns (retries, timeouts, mTLS, circuit breaking, tracing) in every service, the mesh handles them **transparently via sidecar proxies**.

The key insight: move networking concerns out of application code into the platform.

sections.title: Service Mesh Architecture (Istio)
sections.content:
graph TB
    subgraph Control Plane
        Istiod[Istiod\nConfig + Cert Authority]
    end
    subgraph Service A Pod
        AppA[App Container]
        EnvoyA[Envoy Sidecar]
    end
    subgraph Service B Pod
        AppB[App Container]
        EnvoyB[Envoy Sidecar]
    end
    Istiod -->|push config| EnvoyA
    Istiod -->|push config| EnvoyB
    AppA <-->|localhost| EnvoyA
    AppB <-->|localhost| EnvoyB
    EnvoyA <-->|mTLS| EnvoyB
    style Istiod fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style EnvoyA fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style EnvoyB fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Sidecar Proxy Pattern

Every service pod gets an **Envoy proxy sidecar** injected automatically (via webhook). All inbound and outbound traffic goes through the proxy — the application is unaware.

```yaml
# Istio injects this automatically into your pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: my-service      # your app
    image: my-service:v1
  - name: istio-proxy     # injected by Istio
    image: istio/proxyv2:1.20
    # intercepts all traffic via iptables rules
```

### What Envoy handles automatically:
- **Mutual TLS (mTLS)** — encrypt all service-to-service traffic
- **Retries + timeouts** — configure once, applies to all services
- **Circuit breaking** — stop cascading failures
- **Load balancing** — L7 aware (weighted routing, locality)
- **Observability** — automatic traces, metrics, access logs

sections.title: mTLS — Mutual TLS Between Services
sections.content:
graph LR
    subgraph Without mesh
        S1[Service A] -->|plaintext| S2[Service B]
        Note1[Anyone on the network can read traffic]
    end
    subgraph With Istio mTLS
        E1[Envoy A] -->|TLS encrypted\ncertificate verified both ways| E2[Envoy B]
        Note2[Identity verified\nTraffic encrypted\nNo code changes]
    end
    style E1 fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style E2 fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style Note1 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style Note2 fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Traffic Management

Istio gives fine-grained control over traffic routing:

```yaml
# VirtualService — routing rules
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1alpha3
kind: VirtualService
metadata:
  name: reviews
spec:
  http:
  - match:
    - headers:
        x-user-beta:
          exact: "true"
    route:
    - destination:
        host: reviews
        subset: v2      # beta users → v2
  - route:
    - destination:
        host: reviews
        subset: v1      # everyone else → v1
        weight: 90
    - destination:
        host: reviews
        subset: v3
        weight: 10      # 10% canary to v3
```

This enables: **canary deployments**, **A/B testing**, **blue-green**, **fault injection**, **traffic mirroring**.

sections.title: Service mesh adds complexity
sections.content: A service mesh is powerful but adds significant operational overhead: more components to monitor, debug, and upgrade. Start without a mesh. Add one when you have 10+ services and the cross-cutting concerns (mTLS, retries, observability) are becoming a repeated per-service burden.

sections.question: What is the primary role of the sidecar proxy in a service mesh?
options: It replaces the application container with a more efficient version
It intercepts all network traffic to/from the application and applies cross-cutting concerns like mTLS, retries, and observability
It stores service configuration in a distributed cache
It monitors application health and restarts it if it crashes
sections.explanation: The sidecar proxy (Envoy in Istio) is injected alongside the application container and intercepts all inbound/outbound traffic via iptables rules. The application is unaware of it. The proxy handles mTLS encryption, retries, timeouts, circuit breaking, and automatically generates distributed traces — without any code changes.
